stem: A research team wants complete control over readout-error correction: they need the untouched per-shot measurement outcomes so they can apply their own calibration matrix afterward. Which statement about SamplerV2 is correct?

options.text: SamplerV2 applies no readout-error mitigation to its output by default; it returns the raw per-shot bitstrings (a BitArray), so any custom mitigation can be done in post-processing
SamplerV2 always applies TREX readout mitigation, so obtaining raw outcomes is impossible without editing the runtime source
You must set `resilience_level = 0` on the sampler to disable its built-in mitigation before raw counts are returned
Bypassing mitigation requires switching to EstimatorV2, which is the only primitive that exposes raw data

explanation.correct: The Sampler primitive is deliberately low-level: it returns the sampled bitstrings as a BitArray with no post-processing correction of measurement error applied by default. That raw per-shot data is exactly what you need to implement your own mitigation (e.g. build and apply a readout-calibration/confusion matrix, or use a package like mthree). Error suppression (dynamical decoupling) and twirling can be opted into, but they do not alter the counts into 'mitigated' values.

distractors.B: TREX is an Estimator resilience technique for expectation values; the Sampler does not apply it, so raw outcomes are simply what you already get.
distractors.C: The Sampler has no `resilience_level` at all, so there is nothing to set to 0 — the returned counts are already raw.
distractors.D: It is the Sampler, not the Estimator, that exposes per-shot samples. The Estimator returns processed expectation values, which is the opposite of raw data.
